input.userRequest: Recommend how much tracing and retry behavior to keep for the next demo run.

messages.content: You are Briefing Genie, a concise research briefing generator. Use only the provided synthetic source packet. Cite claims with source ids exactly as shown, such as A1 or B2. Do not invent citation ids, facts, or private data. Return a concise briefing suitable for a strategy review. Before writing each claim, verify that its full sentence is directly supported by its citations. Prefer the strongest direct citation for the decision point over a merely related citation. If a detail is only partially supported, narrow the claim or move the uncertainty to open questions. Avoid broad quantifiers, causal claims, and rollout recommendations unless the cited source directly supports them.

User request: Recommend how much tracing and retry behavior to keep for the next demo run.

Source packet: Cost And Latency Budget Packet

Packet summary: Run data, finance guidance, and user feedback about trace depth, retries, and demo responsiveness.

Sources:

[S1] Run telemetry
Type: telemetry report
The candidate prompt reduced unsupported claims but increased median token usage by 11%. Latency improved by 6% because fewer generations needed manual reruns, even though each retained trace is longer.

Telemetry compares the candidate prompt with the baseline over the visible eval set. Token usage rose because the candidate retained more claim-level evidence, but fewer outputs needed manual reruns. The latency improvement came from fewer repeated attempts, not from shorter single generations.

[S2] Finance guidance
Type: finance note
Finance approved demo-quality eval runs up to the reference target cost budget if the report shows why extra tokens reduce review time. Anything above that budget needs a smaller case batch or stricter retry cap.

Finance accepted the reference target cost budget for demo-quality runs because better traces can reduce reviewer time. The approval is conditional: if the run exceeds that budget, the team should reduce batch size, cap retries, or summarize traces more aggressively. The note does not approve unlimited trace retention.

[S3] Reviewer feedback
Type: reviewer feedback
Reviewers said long traces are useful only when they are summarized next to the claim that failed. Raw provider payloads alone slowed review because people had to search for the relevant evidence.

Reviewers liked traces when they were attached to the failed claim and cited source. Raw payload dumps slowed them down because they had to search for the relevant turn. The requested improvement is a short trace summary near each evaluator finding.

[S4] Demo rehearsal
Type: rehearsal note
The live demo felt responsive when single briefings finished under 10 seconds and eval progress showed the current case. Stakeholders were tolerant of longer full runs if progress and artifact paths updated continuously.

The rehearsal note separates product responsiveness from lab-run duration. A single briefing felt live-demo safe under 10 seconds. Full eval runs could take longer if the UI showed the current case, elapsed time, and artifact path so observers understood progress.

[S5] Retry policy draft
Type: policy draft
The retry policy draft proposes a small retry cap for demo eval runs. It allows one automatic retry for transient provider errors and requires a manifest entry when retries change cost or latency. The policy rejects silent repeated generations because they make cost harder to explain during review.

[S6] Trace summary mock
Type: trace mock
The trace summary mock shows a short explanation beside each failed claim: selected source ids, missing evidence, retry count, and final output path. Reviewers preferred that mock to raw provider payloads. The mock keeps detailed traces on disk while making the dashboard readable during a live demo.

output.title: Recommendation: tracing + retries for next demo run
output.summary: Keep claim-level trace summaries in the UI and store full traces on disk; cap retries tightly (one automatic retry for transient errors) and make retry/cost impacts explicit in the manifest. This matches reviewer preferences, supports finance’s conditional budget approval, and preserves demo responsiveness via readable progress and artifacts.

claims.text: Candidate prompt runs used 11% more median tokens while improving latency by 6% due to fewer repeated attempts, not shorter single generations.

Finance approves demo-quality eval runs up to the reference target cost budget when the report explains how extra tokens reduce review time; runs above that budget require reducing batch size, capping retries, or summarizing traces more aggressively.
Reviewers found long traces useful when summarized next to the failed claim, while raw provider payloads alone slowed review because evidence was hard to locate.

The live demo felt responsive when single briefings finished under 10 seconds and eval progress showed the current case; stakeholders tolerated longer full runs when progress and artifact paths updated continuously.

The retry policy draft proposes a small retry cap for demo eval runs: one automatic retry for transient provider errors, plus a manifest entry when retries change cost or latency, and it rejects silent repeated generations.

openQuestions: Is the next demo run expected to stay within the reference target cost budget, or should we pre-emptively reduce case batch size to avoid exceeding it? (Impacts how aggressive trace summarization and retry caps need to be.)
What portion of current latency risk is from transient provider errors vs. model/evidence failures? (Determines how often the single automatic retry would trigger.)
Do we need full raw provider payloads visible in the dashboard during the demo, or is 'full traces on disk + summarized per-claim trace in UI' sufficient for stakeholders?
What is the current p95 single-briefing runtime, and can it reliably stay under 10 seconds with claim-level summaries enabled?
output.recommendation: Tracing: keep per-claim trace summaries in the dashboard (selected source ids, missing evidence, retry count, output path) and keep detailed traces on disk rather than dumping raw provider payloads into the UI. This aligns with reviewer feedback that summarized traces accelerate review while raw payload dumps slow it down (S3), and matches the preferred mock pattern of a readable dashboard with full traces available off-UI (S6). Retries: keep a strict demo retry cap—one automatic retry for transient provider errors only—and record any retry-driven cost/latency changes in the manifest; avoid silent repeated generations (S5). Cost/latency guardrails: aim to remain within the reference target cost budget and, if projected to exceed it, respond by reducing batch size, tightening retry behavior, or summarizing traces more aggressively (S2). Demo responsiveness: prioritize keeping single briefings under ~10 seconds and show continuous progress/current case plus artifact paths to make longer runs acceptable to observers (S4).
